elements.sent-2411-001: The Gregory-Laflamme (GL) instability is a fundamental and ubiquitous phenomenon in the physics of higher-dimensional black holes, where horizons that extend much more in some directions than in others become unstable to growing ripples along the longer directions \cite{Gregory:1993vy,Gregory:1994bj .}
elements.sent-2411-002: In this article we are motivated by the close relationship between black holes and the highly excited states of fundamental strings---made concrete in the black hole/string correspondence of \cite{Susskind:1993ws,Horowitz:1996nw,Horowitz:1997jc ---to explore this phenomenon within string theory.}
elements.sent-2411-003: The deep non-linear growth of the GL instability has been shown to lead to the appearance of naked singularities on the horizon, which makes this system a natural setting to investigate how string theory may resolve these singularities.
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Black-string naked singularities and stringy effects.
elements.sent-2411-004: Considering the illustrative case of a thin, long, neutral black string, we can find naked curvature singularities in two different circumstances:.
elements.sent-2411-005: Late time evolution of generic initial perturbations of the unstable black string, which grow until the horizon pinches off at a thin neck, thus violating cosmic censorship \cite{Lehner:2010pn,Figueras:2022zkg .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Evolution along the space of static solutions of increasingly non-uniform black strings.
elements.sent-2411-007: This branch of solutions arises from the static zero-mode at the threshold of the instability \cite{Wiseman:2002zc,Kleihaus:2006ee,Sorkin:2006wp,Harmark:2007md,Figueras:2012xj,Kalisch:2016fkm,Kalisch:2017bin,Cardona:2018shd .}
elements.sent-2411-008: When followed non-linearly, the non-uniformity of the black strings grows until a solution is reached where the horizon pinches at a conifold where the curvature diverges \cite{Kol:2002xz,Kol:2003ja .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The two scenarios, time-dependent and static, are interesting in themselves and have been thoroughly analyzed, but time evolution can be computationally very costly while the construction of static solutions is much easier.
elements.sent-2411-010: Thus, the latter has often been regarded as the simpler means of obtaining information about black string naked singularities, even though their detailed structure can be quite different in the two cases.
elements.sent-2411-011: The black hole/string correspondence suggests a natural way of dealing with these singularities.
elements.sent-2411-012: A black hole whose horizon curvature is near the string scale is expected to morph into a highly excited string ball.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the two scenarios above, when the curvature along the horizon reaches the string scale, a transition of roughly this kind should prevent the appearance of naked singularities.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the time-evolving situation, string theory should control the further evolution of the string ball and provide a plausible mechanism by which the black string is severed into separate horizons (see Fig.~\ref{fig:1 ~left).}
elements.sent-2411-015: At first glance, it would seem very difficult to make precise this picture of the evolution, due to the significant time-dependence involved, but, remarkably, there are good indications suggesting that the evolution slows down as the string scale is approached, making it more amenable to study (see Sec.~\ref{sec:notnear ) .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{images/DynPinch.png} \quad \includegraphics[width=0.46\textwidth]{images/StatPinch.png} \end{center} \caption{ Left: Dynamical formation of a pinch during the evolution of the black string instability. When the curvature at the pinching neck reaches the string scale, it is expected to transition into a stringy regime (fuzzy green). In this article, we describe this stringy phase and propose that it evolves by evaporating into radiation at the Hagedorn temperature, thus completing the breakup of the black string. Right: Pinching along the solution space of static non-uniform black strings. Although we expect that string theory smooths out the singular conical pinch, our understanding of this resolution remains incomplete.} \label{fig:1}
elements.sent-2411-016: On the other hand, the curvature pinch on the highly non-uniform static black strings seems to offer a loosely similar picture (see Fig.~\ref{fig:1 ~right).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Ideally, string theory would resolve the singular cone geometry that locally models the pinch.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, it seems difficult to tackle this problem with current methods (see Sec.~\ref{sec:statpinch ).}
elements.sent-2411-019: We will show that progress in understanding stringy effects in the GL setup can be made if we consider that the black string reaches string-scale thickness along a portion of its horizon.
elements.sent-2411-020: Then we can regard that part of the black string as having transitioned into a `string-ball string', that is, an object made of highly excited string that extends along a linear direction.
elements.sent-2411-021: We may envision it as the result of starting with a black string and lowering the gravitational coupling until the curvature of the horizon reaches the string scale.
elements.sent-2411-022: Although this system may not exactly match the ones mentioned above, we will extract from it useful lessons about the stringy aspects of the GL instability, as well as about the broader connections between black holes and self-gravitating fundamental strings.
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, we will be able to investigate in detail the properties of the stringy counterparts of non-uniform black strings and learn about their stability.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our approach is based on the formalism for self-gravitating string states near the Hagedorn temperature developed by Horowitz and Polchinski (HP) in \cite{Horowitz:1997jc .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Highly excited string states are described in terms of a mean field---the thermal winding scalar---that couples to the Newtonian potential of weak gravitational fields.
elements.sent-2411-026: Under its own gravity, the thermal scalar condenses to form `string balls' that solve the same equations as static Newtonian boson stars \cite{Friedberg:1986tp,Jones:1995wb .}
elements.sent-2411-027: There are two important differences with the latter: (i) the thermal scalar is intrinsically a field in Euclidean space and can only describe finite-temperature equilibrium configurations---static or stationary, but not time-dependent ones.
elements.sent-2411-028: As mentioned, this may pose less of a problem for our purposes than it initially appears and, in any case, serves our goal of constructing and analyzing non-uniform HP solutions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Ref.~\cite{Chen:2021dsw argued that HP balls have a negative Euclidean mode analogous to that of the Euclidean Schwarzschild solution.}
elements.sent-2411-030: It has long been known that this mode can be alternatively identified as the static zero-mode at the threshold of the GL instability \cite{Gregory:1987nb,Reall:2001ag , and this is the case for black strings and HP strings alike.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This suggests that HP strings may exhibit GL instabilities.
elements.sent-2411-032: While the HP formalism can not directly uncover unstable modes, it motivates us to further investigate this possibility using all available tools.
elements.sent-2411-033: We will explicitly obtain the zero modes for HP strings and find a notable agreement between their wavelength values and those of black string zero modes.
elements.sent-2411-034: As we will discuss shortly, uniform HP strings below the Hagedorn temperature exist only in spatial dimensions $d=4,5,6$ (spacetime dimension $D = d+1=5,6,7$).
elements.sent-2411-035: For these cases the numerical results for the zero-mode wavelength, in appropriate units, are \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lstar} L_*= \begin{cases} 7.96,\ 4.84,\ 2.77 \qquad HP strings\,, 7.17,\ 4.95,\ 3.98 \qquad black strings\,. \end{cases}\end{equation} Despite some ambiguity in the comparison that we will address, the closeness between the two sets of values is noteworthy, given the markedly different form of the equations governing each system.
elements.sent-2411-036: Next, we move beyond this perturbative analysis to construct the branch of non-uniform HP strings in a Kaluza-Klein circle of length $L$, continuing until the non-uniformity is so large that the solutions are better viewed as HP balls localized within the Kaluza-Klein circle.
elements.sent-2411-037: Here, we observe one example of a stringy resolution of GL singularities: unlike the transition from black strings to localized black holes, which involves a singular change in horizon topology, HP strings and localized balls are smoothly connected, with no discontinuity.
elements.sent-2411-038: In contrast to the similarity in \eqref{eq:Lstar for the linearized perturbations, the non-linear regime of HP solutions in a KK circle exhibits a phase structure that is qualitatively distinct from that of black strings.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The properties of HP balls show a marked dependence on the spatial dimension $d$.
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, at any temperature below the Hagedorn limit they exist only for $d=3,4,5$ because, in higher dimensions, the gravitational potential that binds them becomes too short-ranged to counterbalance the outward pressure of the condensate \cite{Horowitz:1997jc .\footnote{In $d=6$ there exist solutions at the Hagedorn temperature \cite{Balthazar:2022szl,Balthazar:2022hno which we will occasionally mention.}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We have found evidence of their existence also in $d=7,8,9$ and possibly higher.
elements.sent-2411-042: As a result, uniform HP strings are limited to $d=4,5,6$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Non-uniform HP configurations reveal this tension: as non-uniformity increases, the solutions in $d=4$ and $5$ interpolate between a uniform string and a localized ball.
elements.sent-2411-044: However, in $d=6$, the condensate becomes less localized and spreads out, since no solution with exponential fall-off exists \cite{Balthazar:2022szl .}
elements.sent-2411-045: In this case, it is also necessary to consider a phase of nearly free string balls that are too large to be gravitationally bound.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: From thermodynamics to strings at a pinch.
elements.sent-2411-046: By examining their thermodynamic properties, we find that the relative dominance of the non-uniform and uniform solutions differs between $d=4,5$ and $d=6$, as well as between the microcanonical and canonical ensembles:.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the microcanonical ensemble of fixed mass and fixed circle length, uniform self-gravitating HP strings are preferred in $d=4,5$, while in $d=6$ the preferred phases are free strings.
elements.sent-2411-048: Non-uniform HP strings, including localized HP balls, are never the dominant microcanonical phase.
elements.sent-2411-049: In the canonical ensemble of fixed temperature and circle length, non-uniform HP solutions always dominate in $d=4,5$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In $d=6$ uniform HP strings are the dominant phase, except very close to the Hagedorn temperature, where the description is in terms of free strings.
elements.sent-2411-051: Most of these features are hinted at by comparing the analytically known properties of uniform strings and localized balls, as we will do in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-053: To properly analyze the thermodynamic competition between uniform and non-uniform phases, we will explicitly construct the latter.
elements.sent-2411-054: Computing their thermodynamic properties then allows us to draw the conclusions outlined above.
elements.sent-2411-055: With these results in hand, we will revisit the questions posed at the beginning of the article and propose how string theory may resolve the pinching singularities in the evolution of unstable black strings.
elements.sent-2411-056: We will argue that the results of the HP formalism, although inherently limited to static configurations, can be meaningfully applied in the final stages of the GL instability.
elements.sent-2411-057: This is supported by recent evidence (\cite{Figueras:2025 , unpublished) that the pinching evolution of black strings decelerates as $\alpha'$ corrections to the gravitational action become significant and the black hole/string changeover is near.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Therefore, the static HP solutions we have constructed can be the crucial transitional configuration leading to the non-singular endpoint of the GL instability.
elements.sent-2411-059: The remainder of the article is organized as follows: The next section provides an elementary, heuristic study of the thermodynamic competition between uniform HP strings and localized balls, concluding, in particular, that the latter never dominate the microcanonical ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-060: This sets the stage for our subsequent detailed analysis, which begins in Section~\ref{sec:hpstrings with the introduction of the HP framework and the construction of its solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-061: In Section~\ref{sec:entropy_results we examine the thermodynamic phases and their stability in both the microcanonical and canonical ensembles.}
elements.sent-2411-062: These results are then employed in Section~\ref{sec:resolution to argue for a stringy resolution of the GL singularities.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion , we conclude with brief remarks on open directions for future work.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Readers primarily interested in our proposal for resolving the GL singularity may wish to begin with Section~\ref{sec:basics , and then skip to the final paragraphs of Section~\ref{sec:stabcrit , which summarize the key information needed for the arguments presented in Section~\ref{sec:resolution .}}}

elements.section-2411-002: Thermodynamic comparison between HP strings and balls
elements.sent-2411-066: In \cite{Gregory:1993vy,Gregory:1994bj a simple argument was given for why black strings might be expected to evolve into localized black holes.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The entropy of a black hole in $D=d+1$ dimensions takes the form \begin{equation}\label{SMbh} S_{BH}=c_d\, M^{\frac{d-1}{d-2}}\,\end{equation} (the value of $c_d$ is not needed for the argument).
elements.sent-2411-068: Now we imagine that this black hole is localized in a circle of length $L$, and neglect the finite-size distortions that would modify the entropy formula.
elements.sent-2411-069: Next, we consider a black string in the same number of dimensions, obtained by uniformly translating a $(d-1)$-dimensional black hole.
elements.sent-2411-070: The formula \eqref{SMbh applies after replacing $d\to d-1$ and scaling $S\to S/L$ and $M\to M/L$, so the entropy is \begin{equation}\label{SMst} S_{BS}=c_{d-1} M^{\frac{d-2}{d-3}} L^{-\frac1{d-3}}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-071: It is now clear that if we compare a black hole and a black string of the same mass, then for $L$ sufficiently larger than $M$, it will be entropically favorable for a black string to transition into a localized black hole.
elements.sent-2411-072: Note that the argument indicates a thermodynamic instability but it does not imply a dynamic instability, namely, there may not be any classical evolution connecting the two systems.
elements.sent-2411-073: If the black string is unstable, it may happen that the classical evolution takes it to a stable non-uniform black string, and not all the way down to a fully localized black hole (which would involve a singular transition).
elements.sent-2411-074: There is strong evidence that the actual evolution and endpoint of the instability depends on the number of dimensions \cite{Emparan:2018bmi .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Let us now apply the same simple argument to HP balls and strings using the results of \cite{Chen:2021dsw , which we will rederive below, in the form \begin{equation}\label{SMHPb} S_{b} = \beta_H M + g_d \frac{d-4}{d-6}M^{\frac{d-6}{d-4}}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The factor $g_d$ is positive in any $d$ and multiplies the self-interaction corrections to the entropy $S=\beta_H M$ of the free string, at a temperature slightly below the Hagedorn temperature $T_H=\beta_H^{-1}\sim 1/\sqrt{\alpha'}$.
elements.sent-2411-077: In $d=4$ no correction is obtained within the approximations of the model, which gives ball solutions with the same mass independently of the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-078: We assume again that this equation provides a good estimation of the entropy of an HP ball localized in a long enough circle $L$.
elements.sent-2411-079: For a uniform HP string, the same reasoning as above gives \begin{equation}\label{SMHPs} S_{s} = \beta_H M + g_{d-1} \frac{d-5}{d-7}M^{\frac{d-7}{d-5}}L^{\frac2{d-5}}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-080: Recall that HP strings exist only in $d=4,5,6$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Again, in $d=5$ there is no correction to the entropy withing this model.
elements.sent-2411-082: We can now compare the entropies of an HP ball and an HP string of the same mass.
elements.sent-2411-083: We observe that in $d=4$ the correction for a string is $\sim +1/L^2$ while the ball receives no correction.
elements.sent-2411-084: So, for any non-zero length $L$, the string is always more entropic than the ball and therefore will be thermodynamically preferred.
elements.sent-2411-085: In $d=5$ the ball entropy is corrected by a negative term, while the string receives no correction.
elements.sent-2411-086: So, again, the uniform HP string is thermodynamically favored.
elements.sent-2411-087: This suggests that, at least in $d=4,5$, long, uniform HP strings are unlikely to break up into rounded string balls.
elements.sent-2411-088: This conclusion is in stark contrast with the behavior of black objects, which are always expected to break up if they are sufficiently long.
elements.sent-2411-089: In $d=6$ the situation is more complicated.
elements.sent-2411-090: The string entropy is reduced by $\sim -L^2$ and the approximations break down for large enough $L$.
elements.sent-2411-091: But in this dimension the string cannot evolve into a localized HP ball, since the latter does not exist at any temperature below the Hagedorn limit.
elements.sent-2411-092: Instead, very large, puffed-up balls have been found in $d=6$ \cite{Balthazar:2022hno at the Hagedorn temperature and with entropy $S\simeq \beta_H M$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Therefore these will dominate over uniform strings.
elements.sent-2411-094: In $d>6$, where there are no self-gravitating string balls at any temperature, the relevant configurations are presumably free-string balls, again with Hagedorn temperature and entropy.
elements.sent-2411-095: The canonical ensemble of phases at fixed temperature is rarely discussed for black objects in this context, since it gives the same conclusions as the microcanonical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-096: Interestingly, for HP strings and balls the situation turns out to be reversed in the two ensembles.
elements.sent-2411-097: The free energy for temperatures near the Hagedorn limit can be approximated by \begin{equation}\label{eq:freeF} F=M-TS\simeq \left(1-\frac{T}{T_H}\right) M - T_H \delta S\,,\end{equation} where $\delta S= S-\beta_H M$ is the self-gravitation correction to the entropy.
elements.sent-2411-098: For the HP ball, this gives \begin{equation}\label{eq:Fball} F_b=\frac{2}{6-d}g_d^\frac{d-4}{2}\left(1-\frac{T}{T_H}\right)^\frac{6-d}{2}\,,\end{equation} and for the uniform HP string, \begin{equation}\label{eq:Fustring} F_s=L\frac{2}{7-d}g_{d-1}^\frac{d-5}{2}\left(1-\frac{T}{T_H}\right)^\frac{7-d}{2}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-100: Given that the dimension factors are positive, when $L$ is large enough the HP ball will have lower free energy and thus dominate in $d=4,5$.
elements.sent-2411-101: For $d=6$, the ball solution is not well defined at any $T<T_H$.
elements.sent-2411-102: The apparent blow-up of $F_b$ seems to indicate that non-uniformity increases the free energy.
elements.sent-2411-103: In the next two sections, we will confirm these preliminary conclusions with a detailed study of non-uniform HP strings and their thermodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-104: The highly excited states of string theory near the Hagedorn temperature can be collectively described, in the Euclidean time formalism, by an effective mean-field $\chi$.
elements.sent-2411-105: This is the winding mode of the string around the Euclidean time circle, which becomes almost massless when its length is $\beta\simeq \beta_H$ \cite{Polchinski:1985zf,Atick:1988si .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Being light, this field must be added to the effective action of string theory at low energies, which also contains the graviton and dilaton.
elements.sent-2411-107: The coupling between the latter and $\chi$ allows to describe self-gravitating configurations of highly-excited strings, often called string balls or string stars \cite{Horowitz:1997jc .}

elements.sent-2411-347: The first one is that the existence of a GL zero mode of the uniform string is often accompanied by a dynamical instability at longer wavelengths.

elements.sent-2411-350: In a second line of reasoning, it is a general property of uniform extended systems that the sign of their specific heat $C$ has a direct bearing on their dynamical stability.
elements.sent-2411-351: In the limit when the system is very long, its density fluctuations at long wavelengths are hydrodynamic sound waves with an effective sound speed $v_s^2=s/C$, where $s$ is the entropy density.
elements.sent-2411-352: If $C<0$, these fluctuations do not propagate as sound but instead grow exponentially large.\footnote{This argument \cite{Emparan:2012be justifies the Gubser-Mitra correlated stability conjecture \cite{Gubser:2000mm in the hydrodynamic regime.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-008: Dynamical pinch: The Singularity is not near
elements.sent-2411-376: As discussed at the end of Sec.~\ref{sec:stabcrit , if we start with a static uniform HP string, the Gregory-Laflamme instability is quenched.}
elements.sent-2411-377: Can this be relevant for the black string instability at late times, when the curvature along the thinning horizon reaches the string scale? The classical evolution of the unstable black string in Einstein gravity has been studied in detail through numerical simulations in \cite{Lehner:2010pn,Figueras:2022zkg .}
elements.sent-2411-378: At late times, a thin neck forms, shrinking at a rate that is approximately linear in time.
elements.sent-2411-379: This rapid contraction suggests that once the stringy regime is reached, the time dependence may be too strong to rely on the static HP theory.
elements.sent-2411-380: Remarkably, there is evidence that the instability tapers off as the stringy stage is approached, with the evolution significantly slowing down.
elements.sent-2411-381: This is consistent with our string theory analysis, bootstrapping the use of the HP solutions in the stringy regime.
elements.sent-2411-382: This evidence comes from an impressive study of the deep non-linear evolution of the black string instability in the Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet (EGB) theory in five dimensions by Figueras, Kovács and Yao \cite{Figueras:2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-383: This theory is not a proper truncation of the low-energy effective string theory to leading order in $\alpha'$, which in this context would at least require the introduction of the dilaton \cite{Callan:1988hs,Myers:1987qx .\footnote{The axion and the two-form gauge field can be set to zero in our problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: However, the dilaton is only sourced at quadratic order in $\alpha'$, so EGB may be a good proxy to string theory (plus, importantly, the theory admits a well-posed initial value formulation \cite{Papallo:2017qvl,Kovacs:2020ywu,Figueras:2024bba ).}
elements.sent-2411-385: Of course, very close to the string scale, the higher-order corrections in the effective theory will also become important, meaning that one must change to a proper string theory description, and this is indeed where we hope our results are of use.
elements.sent-2411-386: The starting point in \cite{Figueras:2025 is a slight perturbation of a uniform black string with a thickness much larger than $\sqrt{\alpha'}$ (where, in a slight abuse, $\alpha'$ denotes the EGB squared-length coupling).}
elements.sent-2411-387: Without delving into the intricate details of the evolution that follows, what matters here is that, initially, the non-uniformity grows until a thin neck forms on the horizon, as expected.
elements.sent-2411-388: However, when the thickness approaches the string length $\sqrt{\alpha'}$, the process slows down and eventually halts, resulting in a long stretch of stable, thin, uniform black string.
elements.sent-2411-389: At this point, the effects of the GB term dominate, causing the effective theory to break down.
elements.sent-2411-390: What is relevant for our discussion is the strong deceleration of the evolution.
elements.sent-2411-391: Crucially, this slowdown occurs only when the sign of the GB coupling matches that of string theory; if the sign is reversed, the pinching continues to the bitter end.
elements.sent-2411-392: So far, the authors of \cite{Figueras:2025 have only performed evolutions in five dimensions within the framework of the EGB theory.}
elements.sent-2411-393: It remains to be seen whether these results hold qualitatively in other dimensions or with other string-inspired corrections.
elements.sent-2411-394: Although tentative and with all the caveats mentioned above, these results allow us to piece together the available evidence into a coherent picture.
elements.sent-2411-395: We interpret them as suggesting that, once the system has entered the stringy regime, the HP theory can reasonably serve as a guide for its subsequent evolution.
elements.sent-2411-396: Indeed, our analysis indicates that no further classical pinching evolution occurs in the neck: in $d=4,5$, the uniform HP string appears to remain dynamically stable, while in $d \geq 6$, it expands into a ball of free string.
elements.paragraph-2411-016: Non-singular endpoint of the GL instability.
elements.sent-2411-397: Once it reaches this stringy stage, how does the system continue its evolution? Let us first consider the case of $d=4,5$.
elements.sent-2411-398: While classically stable, when $g$ is non-zero the HP strings are expected to quantum-mechanically decay through radiation emission near the Hagedorn temperature.
elements.sent-2411-399: They will continue to evaporate in this way until their mass reaches the lower bound in \eqref{eq:Mbound , at which point their self-gravity becomes negligible and they are better described as free string balls.}
elements.sent-2411-400: These free string balls will continue to radiate at the Hagedorn temperature \cite{Amati:1999fv , until the evaporation process is complete.}
elements.sent-2411-401: At this point, the black string finally fragments into separate pieces, bringing the GL evolution to a close.
elements.sent-2411-402: How long does the HP phase take to evaporate? While no concrete calculations are available (and it is unclear how to perform them), it seems reasonable to expect that, similarly to Hawking emission in black holes, the HP ball radiates at a rate of one quantum of energy $\sim M_s$ in a string time $M_s^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-403: To reach the final free-string phase, at least an $O(1)$ fraction of the initial energy must be radiated by the HP ball, and this will take a time $\propto S M_s^{-1}\gg M_s^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-404: It is unclear whether the evaporation of the free string will continue at the same rate or faster, but the important point is that the entire stringy phase of the GL instability will last a time much longer than if the pinching had continued at the classical evolution rate at the moment of the transition into the stringy regime, $\Gamma \propto M_s$.
elements.sent-2411-405: The duration of the evaporation stage in $d \geq 6$, where free-string balls dominate the stringy phase of the evolution, is somewhat more uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-406: However, for small coupling $g$, the evaporation is likely to still be much slower than the classical shrinking rate, possibly taking a time of the order $\sim S M_s^{-1}$, similar to the behavior in $d=4,5$.
elements.sent-2411-407: This concludes our picture of the GL instability up until string theory brings about the fragmentation of the black string.
elements.sent-2411-408: In the subsequent evolution, possibly analogous to the behavior of fluid jets \cite{Eggers:1997zz , classical gravity will resume governing the dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-409: This is left for future work.
elements.sent-2411-410: Our study of the HP phases and their thermodynamics admits several extensions, e.g., to strings with electric NS charges, such as an F-string charge along the circle direction.
elements.sent-2411-411: This is expected to further enhance the stability of the uniform solution.
elements.sent-2411-412: The situation in $d>6$ also deserves more attention.
elements.sent-2411-413: The most exciting prospects lie in advancing our understanding of how string theory handles the best-attested violations of weak Cosmic Censorship.

elements.sent-2411-529: The reason that UHPS become stable at small mass while black strings do so at large mass is that in both cases those are the regimes where they become fatter, see \eqref{eq:Mrb and \eqref{eq:Mr0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-530: For low masses, $M<M_*$, when the UHPS is thinner, it has lower entropy than the NUHPS and thus is expected to be unstable.

elements.sent-2411-532: Let us now examine a different argument.
elements.sent-2411-533: It is a general property of uniform extended systems that the sign of their specific heat $C$ has a direct bearing on their dynamical stability.
elements.sent-2411-534: In the limit when the system is very long, its density fluctuations at long wavelengths behave as hydrodynamic sound waves, with an effective sound speed $v_s^2=s/C$, where $s$ is the entropy density.
elements.sent-2411-535: If $C<0$ these fluctuations do not propagate as sound but instead grow exponentially.\footnote{This argument \cite{Emparan:2012be justifies the Gubser-Mitra correlated stability conjecture \cite{Gubser:2000mm in the hydrodynamic regime.}}}
elements.sent-2411-536: Since the uniform HP string in $d=4$ has $C_U<0$, a GL instability is expected at very long wavelengths, consistent with the Morse theory analysis above.
elements.sent-2411-537: The UHPS in $d=5$ has infinite specific heat, so no definitive conclusion can be drawn.
elements.sent-2411-538: However, it seems reasonable to conjecture that, when higher-order corrections are included, its behavior will resemble that of $d=4$, with $C_U<0$ and a long-wavelength GL instability.
elements.sent-2411-539: In addition to this, the sign of the specific heat has been argued in \cite{Reall:2001ag to be correlated with the existence of a negative Euclidean mode for fixed-temperature fluctuations---which translates into the GL zero mode.}
elements.sent-2411-540: Then, the fact that $C_U<0$ in $d=4$ aligns well with the existence of the GL zero mode, and also supports the expectation that corrections in $d=5$ will yield $C_U<0$, also in line with the expected GL instability at long wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-541: However, the arguments based on the sign of the specific heat become puzzling in $d=6$, where $C_U>0$ but a GL zero-mode (and a Euclidean negative mode) still exists, and moreover, Morse theory suggests an instability of the UHPS for wavelengths longer than the critical length.
elements.sent-2411-542: The resolution of this puzzle remains unclear, but we can offer some comments.
elements.sent-2411-543: The GL zero mode is a linearized mode at a finite wavenumber, specifically the critical $k$ found in Sec.~\ref{subsubsection:zeromode .}
elements.sent-2411-544: Its dispersion relation at very small $k$ need not exhibit hydrodynamic behavior; for instance, it could be a tachyonic mode with non-zero imaginary frequency at $k=0$.
elements.sent-2411-545: If this were the case, the sign of $C_U$ would have no bearing on long-wavelength stability.
elements.sent-2411-546: However, this does not resolve the apparent conflict between Morse theory and the arguments in \cite{Reall:2001ag , since these concern the behavior at the critical wavenumber.}
elements.sent-2411-547: One possible explanation is that there may be other modes not captured by the conventional GL analysis, which might lead to phases that could not be identified in our current analysis.
elements.sent-2411-548: Corrections to the EFT are unlikely to solve the problem since they should be small for the UHPS or slightly non-uniform solutions.
elements.sent-2411-549: Furthermore, it is important to remember that the HP framework is intrinsically time-independent, so the previous arguments, while plausible, may not be directly applicable in this context.
elements.sent-2411-550: So far we have discussed only the stability of the uniform HP solutions, but Morse theory also suggests that for fixed mass, the NUHPS should be unstable in $d=4,5$ and stable in $d=6$.
elements.sent-2411-551: Conversely, when fixing the temperature, the NUHPS should be stable in $d=4,5$ and unstable in $d=6$.
elements.sent-2411-552: In all dimensions, the stability at fixed temperature (canonical ensemble) correlates well with the sign of the specific heat $C_{NU}$, but this relationship is reversed if we instead fix the mass (microcanonical ensemble).
elements.sent-2411-553: This differing behavior of the same solution in the two ensembles may be a reflection of the ensemble inequivalence away from the strict thermodynamic limit, which is likely broken by the self-gravitational corrections.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Dynamical pinch: The singularity is not near

elements.sent-2411-583: Of course, very close to the string scale, the higher-order corrections in the effective theory will also become important, meaning that one must change to a proper string theory description, and this is indeed where we hope our study is of use.
elements.sent-2411-584: These results tentatively allow us to assemble the available evidence into a coherent picture.
elements.sent-2411-585: We interpret them as suggesting that once the system enters the stringy regime, the HP theory can reasonably serve as a guide for its subsequent evolution.
elements.sent-2411-586: However, we must acknowledge the limitations of our current understanding.

elements.sent-2411-589: Then, the quasi-stable segment of the pinching black string will transition into a stable UHPS.
elements.sent-2411-590: In $d \geq 6$ it likely expands into a free-string ball.
elements.sent-2411-591: The uncertainties in this picture leave room for more complex evolutions.
elements.sent-2411-592: For instance, the thinning black string might evolve into an unstable HP string.
elements.sent-2411-593: According to Fig.~\ref{fig:all_phases , this would then collapse into a localized black hole, which at a later stage—--presumably through evaporation—--would reach the correspondence transition into a string ball.}
elements.sent-2411-594: Intriguingly, in this scenario the topology-changing evolution from black string to localized black hole would be smoothed out through a classical evolution mediated by a stringy HP phase, without the formation of a singularity.
elements.sent-2411-595: Another unknown is the length of the segment where the pinching black string reaches the string scale.
elements.sent-2411-596: It seems reasonable to assume that this segment would be longer, though not parametrically much longer, than the string-scale length.
elements.sent-2411-597: Notably, in $d=4$ the uniform HP string remains stable up to a length approximately $8$ times its thickness \eqref{L0HP , which is itself larger than the string scale.}
elements.sent-2411-598: Thus, a transition into an HP string segment thick enough to be stable appears plausible.
elements.sent-2411-599: However, one might also envision that the string-scale segment of the black string continues to grow, possibly devolving into the earlier scenario of an unstable HP string collapsing into localized black holes.
elements.sent-2411-600: While we cannot entirely rule out these more intricate evolutionary scenarios, they seem unnaturally contrived.
elements.sent-2411-601: The same mechanism that slows the pinching of the black string near the string scale is more likely to enhance, rather than diminish, the classical stability of the HP string.
elements.sent-2411-602: We hope that future progress into this problem will help refine the picture.
elements.sent-2411-603: For the time being, we adopt the simpler scenario outlined above as our primary framework for how the GL instability progresses into the stringy regime.
